topics.title: Streams API & Functional Interfaces
topics.description: The Streams API (Java 8+) provides a declarative, pipeline-based approach to processing collections. A stream is a sequence of elements supporting sequential and parallel aggregate operations. Combined with functional interfaces (Predicate, Function, Consumer, Supplier) and lambda expressions, Streams enable concise, readable, and composable data transformations without mutating the source. This is Java's answer to functional programming.
topics.story: Think of a Stream like an assembly line in a factory. Raw materials (your collection) enter at one end. Each station on the line performs one transformation — wash, cut, paint, package (filter, map, sort, collect). The line runs only when a product is needed at the end (lazy evaluation). You can run multiple identical lines in parallel (parallel streams). The original raw materials are never changed — the factory produces new products from them.
topics.analogy: Stream pipeline = a SQL query. `stream().filter(p -> p.age > 18).map(Person::getName).collect(toList())` is exactly like `SELECT name FROM persons WHERE age > 18`. The source (table/collection) is unchanged. The pipeline is declarative — you describe WHAT you want, not HOW to iterate. The Streams engine decides the HOW, including whether to go parallel.
topics.memoryAnchor: SIT pipeline — Source (collection, array, Stream.of, Stream.iterate, Stream.generate) → Intermediate ops (LAZY, return Stream: filter, map, flatMap, sorted, distinct, limit, peek) → Terminal op (EAGER, triggers execution: collect, forEach, reduce, count, findFirst, anyMatch, toList). LAZY PROFESSOR rule: intermediate ops are promises, not actions. Nothing runs until a terminal calls. FCPS functional interfaces — Function converts T to R. Predicate screens T to boolean. Consumer eats T (void). Supplier creates T (no input). Collectors.groupingBy / partitioningBy / joining are the power tools. Parallel stream = use with caution, stateless ops only.
topics.firstPrinciples: Before Streams, collection processing required imperative loops with mutable state — error-prone, verbose, and hard to parallelise. Streams separate WHAT (the pipeline definition) from HOW (iteration strategy). Lazy evaluation means intermediate operations build a recipe, not a result — the recipe executes only when a terminal operation demands output. This enables optimisations like short-circuiting (findFirst stops after first match) and fusion (filter + map in a single pass).
topics.bruteForce: Without Streams: List<String> result = new ArrayList<>(); for (Person p : people) { if (p.getAge() > 18) { result.add(p.getName().toUpperCase()); } } Collections.sort(result); — mutable accumulator, multiple passes, difficult to parallelise, verbose boilerplate that obscures the business logic.
topics.optimizedApproach: With Streams: people.stream().filter(p -> p.getAge() > 18).map(p -> p.getName().toUpperCase()).sorted().collect(toList()) — single expression, no mutation, trivially parallelised by changing .stream() to .parallelStream(). The pipeline is lazy: if you add .limit(10), the stream stops after 10 matches without scanning the rest.
topics.whenToUse: Use Streams when: transforming/filtering/aggregating collections (map/filter/reduce patterns), grouping/partitioning data (Collectors.groupingBy), computing statistics (summarizingInt, averagingDouble), or building result strings (Collectors.joining). Avoid Streams when: you need index-based access during iteration, the logic requires mutable shared state, or a simple for-loop is more readable. Never use parallelStream() without measuring — overhead is only worthwhile for large data sets with CPU-bound operations.

examples.title: Stream Pipeline — filter, map, collect
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":19,"lineCode":"people.stream()","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Creates a Stream<Person> backed by the list. No iteration happens yet — lazy."},{"line":20,"lineCode":".filter(p -> p.age() >= 18)","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Registers a filter predicate. Still no iteration — this just adds a stage to the pipeline recipe."},{"line":21,"lineCode":".map(Person::name)","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Registers a mapping function. Stream<Person> becomes Stream<String>. Still lazy."},{"line":22,"lineCode":".sorted()","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Registers sort. Note: sorted() is a stateful intermediate op — must see ALL elements before outputting any."},{"line":23,"lineCode":".collect(Collectors.toList())","variables":{"adults":["Alice","Carol","Eve"]},"phase":"TERMINAL","annotation":"TERMINAL — triggers the entire pipeline. Now the stream iterates: Alice(30)→pass filter→name→sorted buffer. Bob(17)→fail filter→skip. Carol(25)→pass. Dave(15)→fail. Eve(28)→pass. Sort buffer: [Alice,Carol,Eve] → return as List."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A[List of 5 People] -->|.stream| B[Stream source]
    B -->|filter age>=18| C[Alice Carol Eve]
    C -->|map name| D[Alice Carol Eve Strings]
    D -->|sorted| E[Alice Carol Eve sorted]
    E -->|collect toList| F[List Alice Carol Eve]
    G[Bob 17] -->|filter fails| X[❌ skipped]
    H[Dave 15] -->|filter fails| X
examples.pseudocode:
STREAM PIPELINE:
  source     → wraps collection, no iteration yet
  filter(fn) → adds predicate gate (lazy)
  map(fn)    → adds transform stage (lazy)
  sorted()   → adds sort buffer (lazy but stateful)
  collect()  → TERMINAL: triggers pipeline execution

EXECUTION: one element at a time flows through all stages
  element passes all lazily-registered operations in one pass
  (except stateful ops like sorted that buffer)
examples.explanation: Intermediate operations (filter, map, sorted) are lazy — they register stages in a pipeline but do not iterate. The terminal operation (collect, count, findFirst) triggers a single pass through the source. Each element flows through all intermediate stages before the next element is processed. Short-circuit terminals like findFirst stop the pipeline as soon as the condition is satisfied — the rest of the source is never examined.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Data JPA returns List<Entity> — transform with streams to DTOs without loading them all into memory first. Amazon product search: filter by category, map to SearchResult DTO, sort by relevance, limit to page size. Log analysis: Files.lines(path).filter(l -> l.contains('ERROR')).collect(toList()) — streams large files without loading entirely into memory.

examples.title: Collectors — groupingBy, partitioningBy, joining, toMap
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":19,"lineCode":".collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Product::category))","variables":{},"phase":"TERMINAL","annotation":"groupingBy creates a HashMap. For each product, calls Product::category to get the key and appends the product to that key's list. Result: {Fruit=[Apple,Banana], Vegetable=[Carrot,Broccoli], Electronics=[Laptop,Phone]}"},{"line":24,"lineCode":".collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Product::category, Collectors.counting()))","variables":{},"phase":"TERMINAL","annotation":"Downstream collector counting() accumulates count instead of list. Each element increments its category's counter. Result: {Fruit=2, Vegetable=2, Electronics=2}"},{"line":34,"lineCode":".collect(Collectors.partitioningBy(p -> p.price() > 10))","variables":{},"phase":"TERMINAL","annotation":"partitioningBy always produces exactly two keys: true and false. Laptop(999) and Phone(599) go to true. All others to false."},{"line":40,"lineCode":".collect(Collectors.joining(\", \", \"[\", \"]\"))","variables":{},"phase":"TERMINAL","annotation":"joining concatenates String elements with delimiter, wrapping in prefix '[' and suffix ']'. Equivalent to String.join but works in a stream pipeline."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[Stream of Products] --> B[groupingBy category]
    B --> C[Fruit: Apple Banana]
    B --> D[Vegetable: Carrot Broccoli]
    B --> E[Electronics: Laptop Phone]
    A --> F[partitioningBy price > 10]
    F --> G[true: Laptop Phone]
    F --> H[false: Apple Banana Carrot Broccoli]
    A --> I[joining]
    I --> J[Apple Banana Carrot ...]
examples.pseudocode:
groupingBy(classifier):
  Map<K, List<T>> — group elements by key

groupingBy(classifier, downstream):
  Map<K, R> — group then reduce each group
  downstream: counting, averaging, summing, toList, joining...

partitioningBy(predicate):
  Map<Boolean, List<T>> — always two groups

joining(delimiter, prefix, suffix):
  String — concatenates Stream<String>

toMap(keyFn, valueFn):
  Map<K,V> — throws on duplicate keys (use merge fn to handle)
examples.explanation: Collectors are terminal operations that accumulate stream elements into a result container. groupingBy is the most powerful — it accepts a downstream collector to further reduce each group (count, average, sum, join, even another groupingBy for multi-level grouping). partitioningBy is groupingBy with a boolean predicate, always producing exactly two groups. toMap throws IllegalStateException on duplicate keys — use the three-argument form with a merge function to resolve conflicts.
examples.realWorldUse: Dashboard analytics: groupingBy(order::getMonth, summarizingDouble(Order::getAmount)) gives monthly revenue in one pass. Report generation: joining(",") to build CSV rows. API response building: toMap(user::getId, User::getName) for ID-to-name lookup maps. Spring Batch: partitioningBy to split records into valid/invalid before processing.

examples.title: Functional Interfaces — Predicate, Function, Consumer, Supplier
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":11,"lineCode":"Predicate<String> valid = notBlank.and(longEnough);","variables":{},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Predicate.and() returns a new Predicate: s -> notBlank.test(s) && longEnough.test(s). Short-circuits: if notBlank fails, longEnough is never called."},{"line":20,"lineCode":"Function<String, String> pipeline = length.andThen(classify);","variables":{},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Function.andThen() composes: pipeline = s -> classify(length(s)). andThen applies second function to output of first. compose() is reverse order."},{"line":28,"lineCode":"Consumer<String> both = print.andThen(upper);","variables":{},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Consumer.andThen() chains two side-effect operations. Both run for every element. Unlike Function, Consumer returns void — it's for side effects only."},{"line":32,"lineCode":"Supplier<List<String>> listFactory = ArrayList::new;","variables":{},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Supplier has no input, produces a value on demand. ArrayList::new is a constructor reference — each get() call invokes new ArrayList(), giving fresh instances."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Predicate
      P1[notBlank] -->|and| P3[valid]
      P2[longEnough] -->|and| P3
    end
    subgraph Function
      F1[length String→Int] -->|andThen| F3[pipeline String→String]
      F2[classify Int→String] -->|andThen| F3
    end
    subgraph Consumer
      C1[print] -->|andThen| C3[both]
      C2[upper] -->|andThen| C3
    end
    subgraph Supplier
      S1[ArrayList::new] -->|get| S2[new ArrayList]
      S1 -->|get| S3[another new ArrayList]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
Predicate<T>:  boolean test(T t)     — filter, validation
Function<T,R>: R apply(T t)          — transform, convert
Consumer<T>:   void accept(T t)      — side-effect, log, save
Supplier<T>:   T get()              — lazy value, factory

COMPOSITION:
  predicate.and(other)   — both must pass
  predicate.or(other)    — either passes
  predicate.negate()     — flips result
  fn.andThen(after)      — fn then after
  fn.compose(before)     — before then fn
  consumer.andThen(after)— both run in order
examples.explanation: Functional interfaces have exactly one abstract method, enabling lambda assignment. The four core interfaces cover all common patterns: Predicate for boolean decisions, Function for transformations, Consumer for side effects (logging, saving), Supplier for lazy value production. Composition methods (and, andThen, negate) build complex behaviour from simple pieces without subclassing.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Security: Predicate<Authentication> for access rules. Bean Validation: Predicate<T> implementations are the core of custom validators. Jackson: Function<JsonNode, T> for custom deserializers. Retry libraries: Predicate<Throwable> to decide which exceptions trigger a retry. Lazy initialisation: Supplier<HeavyResource> passed to a cache that calls get() only on first access.

examples.title: Method References and flatMap
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":21,"lineCode":"words.stream().map(MethodRefsAndFlatMap::process)","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Static method reference. Equivalent to: map(s -> MethodRefsAndFlatMap.process(s)). The method signature matches Function<String,String>."},{"line":26,"lineCode":"names.stream().map(String::toLowerCase)","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Instance method on arbitrary instance. Equivalent to: map(s -> s.toLowerCase()). String::toLowerCase is Function<String,String> — the stream element becomes 'this' in the method call."},{"line":30,"lineCode":"names.stream().map(prefix::concat)","variables":{"prefix":"Hello, "},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Instance method on specific captured instance. prefix is captured from outer scope. Equivalent to: map(s -> prefix.concat(s)). prefix.concat is called on the same prefix object every time."},{"line":50,"lineCode":".flatMap(o -> o.items.stream())","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"flatMap flattens one level of nesting. Each Order's items List<String> becomes a Stream<String>. flatMap merges all these sub-streams into one continuous Stream<String> — no nested streams in the output."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Method_References
      A[static: Class::staticMethod]
      B[arbitrary: Class::instanceMethod]
      C[specific: obj::instanceMethod]
      D[constructor: Class::new]
    end
    subgraph flatMap
      O1[O1: Apple Banana] -->|flatMap| F[Apple]
      O1 -->|flatMap| G[Banana]
      O2[O2: Carrot] -->|flatMap| H[Carrot]
      O3[O3: Date Elderberry Fig] -->|flatMap| I[Date]
      O3 -->|flatMap| J[Elderberry]
      O3 -->|flatMap| K[Fig]
      F & G & H & I & J & K --> L[Single Stream]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
METHOD REFERENCE KINDS:
  Type::staticMethod     → (args) -> Type.staticMethod(args)
  Type::instanceMethod   → (obj, args) -> obj.instanceMethod(args)
  obj::instanceMethod    → (args) -> obj.instanceMethod(args)
  Type::new              → (args) -> new Type(args)

flatMap vs map:
  map(f)     → Stream<Stream<R>>  (nested)
  flatMap(f) → Stream<R>          (flattened one level)
  Use flatMap when f returns a Stream or Collection
examples.explanation: Method references are shorthand for lambdas that call a single method. The four kinds cover static methods, instance methods called on the stream element (element becomes 'this'), instance methods on a captured object, and constructor calls. flatMap is essential when each element maps to multiple elements — it unwraps one level of nesting. The classic pattern is orders.flatMap(order -> order.getItems().stream()) to get all items across all orders as a single flat stream.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Data: repository.findAll().stream().flatMap(entity -> entity.getTags().stream()).distinct().collect(toList()) — all unique tags across all entities. JSON parsing: jsonArrayNode.elements().asStream() flattened with flatMap for nested arrays. File processing: Files.walk(dir).filter(Files::isRegularFile) uses a static method reference. Collections.sort with Comparator.comparing(Person::getName) uses an instance method reference.

examples.title: Parallel Streams and Custom Collectors
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":21,"lineCode":"List<Integer> numbers = IntStream.rangeClosed(1, 1_000_000)","variables":{"size":1000000},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"IntStream.rangeClosed generates 1..1000000 as a primitive stream. .boxed() converts to Stream<Integer>. rangeClosed includes the endpoint."},{"line":27,"lineCode":"numbers.parallelStream().mapToLong(Integer::longValue).sum()","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"parallelStream() uses the common ForkJoinPool (default: CPU cores - 1 threads). The array is split into chunks, each chunk summed independently, then partial sums merged. Correct because addition is associative."},{"line":38,"lineCode":"List<Integer> unsafeResult = new ArrayList<>();","variables":{},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"ArrayList is NOT thread-safe. parallelStream().forEach(list::add) causes race conditions — multiple threads adding concurrently without synchronisation → corrupted list, missing elements, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"Collector.of(supplier, accumulator, combiner, finisher)","variables":{},"phase":"DEFINE","annotation":"Custom Collector: supplier creates a fresh StringJoiner per thread. accumulator adds each element. combiner merges two StringJoiners (used in parallel mode). finisher converts to the final String result."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[parallelStream source] --> B[ForkJoinPool splits]
    B --> C[Thread 1: 1..250k]
    B --> D[Thread 2: 250k..500k]
    B --> E[Thread 3: 500k..750k]
    B --> F[Thread 4: 750k..1M]
    C & D & E & F -->|partial sums| G[merge: total sum]
    H[Custom Collector]
    H --> I[supplier: new StringJoiner]
    I --> J[accumulator: .add per element]
    J --> K[combiner: merge parallel results]
    K --> L[finisher: .toString]
examples.pseudocode:
PARALLEL STREAM:
  .parallelStream() — ForkJoinPool.commonPool()
  Splits source → processes chunks independently
  Merges results
  SAFE for: stateless ops (filter, map, sorted)
  UNSAFE for: mutable shared state (ArrayList::add)
  ALWAYS safe to collect() — Collectors handle concurrency

CUSTOM COLLECTOR (Collector.of):
  supplier    — creates empty accumulator per thread
  accumulator — folds one element into accumulator
  combiner    — merges two accumulators (parallel only)
  finisher    — converts accumulator to result
examples.explanation: Parallel streams split the source into chunks and process them concurrently in the ForkJoinPool. They are safe when operations are stateless (filter, map) and the merge operation is associative (sum, max, joining). They are unsafe when you add to a shared mutable collection like ArrayList — use Collectors instead which manage thread safety internally. Custom Collectors via Collector.of() enable complex accumulation patterns beyond what the built-in Collectors provide — the combiner makes them parallel-safe.
examples.realWorldUse: Batch data processing: 10M records split across CPU cores with parallelStream(). Custom Collector for building Protobuf messages from stream elements. Image processing: pixel transformations in parallel. NEVER use parallelStream() on I/O-bound work (DB calls, HTTP) — the ForkJoinPool is CPU-only; use CompletableFuture with a separate executor instead. Always benchmark before switching to parallel — overhead is only worthwhile above ~10k elements with CPU-intensive ops.

problems.title: Filter Even Numbers
problems.description: Given `List<Integer> numbers`, return a new list containing only even numbers, in their original order, using streams.
problems.hint3: Do not modify the original list — streams produce a new collection.
problems.constraints: Input list is non-null. May be empty.
problems.editorial: filter() is a stateless intermediate operation — it passes each element through the predicate and drops elements that return false. n % 2 == 0 is the standard even check. The original list is never modified — streams are non-destructive.

problems.title: Map Names to Uppercase
problems.description: Given `List<String> names`, return a new list with every name converted to uppercase using stream's `map()` operation.
problems.hint3: Collect the result into a list with `Collectors.toList()`.
problems.constraints: Names are non-null strings.
problems.editorial: map() transforms each element independently — it is a stateless intermediate operation. String::toUpperCase is an instance method reference on the stream element (equivalent to s -> s.toUpperCase()). map() produces a Stream<String> → collect into List<String>.

problems.title: Sum With reduce()
problems.description: Given `List<Integer> numbers`, compute the sum using `stream().reduce()`. Return 0 for an empty list. Also compute the product using reduce.
problems.hint1: `reduce(0, Integer::sum)` — identity 0, accumulator sums two ints.
problems.hint2: Product: `reduce(1, (a, b) -> a * b)` — identity is 1 for multiplication.
problems.hint3: `reduce(identity, BinaryOperator)` returns T directly (no Optional).
problems.constraints: Numbers fit in int for sum, long for product.
problems.editorial: reduce(identity, accumulator) folds the stream into a single value. The identity is the neutral element (0 for sum, 1 for product). For sum, mapToInt().sum() is more idiomatic and avoids boxing. For product use long to avoid overflow — 10 elements of value 10 already overflows int.

problems.title: Group Students by Grade
problems.description: Given a list of `Student(String name, int score)`, group them into three grade buckets using `Collectors.groupingBy()`: `A` (score >= 90), `B` (score >= 75), `C` (score < 75). Return `Map<String, List<Student>>`.
problems.hint1: Define a helper method `grade(Student s)` returning 'A', 'B', or 'C'.
problems.hint3: The result map may not have all three keys if no student falls in that grade.
problems.constraints: Score range 0–100.
problems.editorial: groupingBy classifies each element by a key function and accumulates elements with the same key into a List. The default downstream collector is Collectors.toList(). The result Map may be missing keys for grades with no students — use getOrDefault(key, List.of()) when accessing.

problems.title: Find First Matching Element
problems.description: Given `List<String> words`, find the first word that starts with the letter 'J' (case-insensitive) and has length > 3. Return `Optional<String>`. If none found, return `Optional.empty()`.
problems.hint3: `findFirst()` returns `Optional<String>` — short-circuits after first match.
problems.constraints: Words are non-null. List may be empty.
problems.editorial: findFirst() is a short-circuit terminal — it stops processing as soon as one element passes all filters. Returns Optional to force callers to handle the 'no match' case. findAny() is an alternative that works better with parallelStream() but has non-deterministic order.

problems.title: Count Distinct Words
problems.description: Given a sentence string, split it into words (split on whitespace), convert to lowercase, remove punctuation (keep only letters), and return the count of distinct words using streams.
problems.hint3: Chain: `Arrays.stream(words).map(...).filter(!blank).distinct().count()`.

testCases.input: To be, or not to be

problems.constraints: Sentence is non-null. Case-insensitive distinct.
problems.editorial: distinct() removes duplicates by calling equals() — so lowercase() must happen before distinct(). filter(!isEmpty) handles punctuation-only tokens. count() is a terminal that returns long. This pipeline shows how streams eliminate the need for a manual Set<String> accumulator.

problems.title: Flatten Nested Lists With flatMap
problems.description: Given `List<List<Integer>> nested`, use `flatMap` to flatten it into a single `List<Integer>` containing all elements in order. Then return the sum of all elements.
problems.hint1: `.flatMap(List::stream)` converts each inner list to a stream and merges all.
problems.hint2: After flatMap you have `Stream<Integer>` — use `.mapToInt().sum()` for the sum.
problems.hint3: flatMap is equivalent to: flatten(nested) = concat(nested.get(0), nested.get(1), ...).
problems.constraints: Inner lists may be empty.
problems.editorial: flatMap(List::stream) is equivalent to flatMap(list -> list.stream()). It converts each inner list into a stream and concatenates all sub-streams into one. map() would give Stream<List<Integer>> (nested); flatMap gives Stream<Integer> (flat). mapToInt().sum() avoids boxing overhead compared to reduce(0, Integer::sum).

problems.title: Top N Elements By Comparator
problems.description: Given `List<Product> products` where `Product(String name, double price)`, return the top N most expensive products as a list, sorted by price descending. Use streams.
problems.hint3: Chain: `stream().sorted(comparator).limit(n).collect(toList())`.
problems.constraints: n <= products.size(). Products non-null.
problems.editorial: sorted() is a stateful intermediate operation — it must buffer all elements before outputting any. reversed() flips the natural ascending order. limit(n) is a short-circuit intermediate — it caps the output. For very large lists where you only need top-N, a PriorityQueue approach is O(n log k) vs sorted's O(n log n), but streams make the code cleaner for typical sizes.

problems.title: Word Frequency Map
problems.description: Given a list of words, build a `Map<String, Long>` of word frequencies using `Collectors.groupingBy` with a counting downstream collector. Then find the most frequent word.
problems.hint3: `Function.identity()` returns the element itself as the grouping key.
problems.constraints: Words non-null. At least one word.
problems.editorial: groupingBy(identity(), counting()) counts occurrences of each unique word. Function.identity() returns the word itself as the key. To find the maximum, stream the entry set and use max(comparingByValue()) — returns Optional<Entry<String,Long>>, so .map(Entry::getKey) extracts just the word.

problems.title: Partition Adults and Minors
problems.description: Given `List<Person> persons` with `name` and `age`, use `Collectors.partitioningBy` to split into adults (age >= 18) and minors. Return `Map<Boolean, List<String>>` where the value is a list of names (not Person objects).
problems.hint3: Result: `true` key = adult names, `false` key = minor names.
problems.constraints: Always produces both true and false keys (partitioningBy guarantees this).
problems.editorial: partitioningBy always produces both keys (true and false) even if one partition is empty — unlike groupingBy which only creates keys for observed values. The downstream Collectors.mapping applies a transform before collecting — here extracting just the name. This avoids a post-processing map step.

problems.title: Stream of Primitives — IntStream Statistics
problems.description: Given an int array, use `IntStream` to compute: sum, average, min, max, and count — all in a single pass using `IntStream.summaryStatistics()`. Also generate an IntStream range and collect it.
problems.hint3: `IntStream.range(0, 10)` generates 0..9. `IntStream.rangeClosed(1,10)` generates 1..10.
problems.constraints: arr non-empty for stats. range: start < end.
problems.editorial: Primitive streams (IntStream, LongStream, DoubleStream) avoid boxing overhead. summaryStatistics() computes count, sum, min, max, average in a single pass — more efficient than five separate terminal operations. IntStream.range(a,b) is exclusive-end; rangeClosed(a,b) is inclusive. .boxed() converts IntStream to Stream<Integer> for collection into a List.

problems.title: Collectors.joining — Build a Formatted String
problems.description: Given `List<String> items`, use `Collectors.joining` to produce: (1) comma-separated string, (2) bracketed with delimiter, (3) a SQL IN clause like `WHERE name IN ('Alice','Bob','Carol')`.
problems.hint3: For SQL: map each name to `'name'` first, then join with `", "`, prefix `WHERE name IN (`, suffix `)`.
problems.constraints: Items are non-null strings.
problems.editorial: Collectors.joining operates on Stream<String> — map to String first if needed. The three-argument form takes delimiter, prefix, suffix. An empty stream with prefix/suffix still produces 'prefix + suffix' (e.g., '[]'). More efficient than manual StringBuilder loops as the Collector manages internal StringJoiner.

problems.title: Compose Predicates Dynamically
problems.description: Build a `ProductFilter` class that lets callers add multiple filter criteria at runtime (min price, max price, category, name contains). Each criterion is a `Predicate<Product>` composed with `and()`. Apply the composed predicate to filter a product list.
problems.hint1: Store predicates in `List<Predicate<Product>>`. Start with `Predicate.not(Objects::isNull)` as base.
problems.hint2: Compose: `predicates.stream().reduce(p -> true, Predicate::and)` — reduces all into one.
problems.hint3: Each `withMinPrice`, `withCategory` etc. adds a predicate to the list and returns `this` for chaining.
problems.constraints: All filter methods are optional — calling none returns all products.
problems.editorial: Predicate.and() short-circuits — if an early predicate fails, later ones are skipped. Starting with `p -> true` as the base identity means zero filters = all pass. This is the Specification/Criteria pattern used by Spring Data's Specification<T> for JPA query building.

problems.title: Multi-Level groupingBy
problems.description: Given `List<Employee(name, department, city, salary)>`, use nested `Collectors.groupingBy` to build a `Map<String, Map<String, Double>>` where the outer key is department, the inner key is city, and the value is the average salary for that department+city combination.
problems.hint3: Result: `map.get("Engineering").get("Bangalore")` → average salary.
problems.constraints: All fields non-null.
problems.editorial: Nested groupingBy: outer key partitions into departments, inner key partitions each department group into cities, downstream averagingDouble computes the average per city group. This produces a two-level Map without any explicit loops or mutation. The pattern extends to three or more levels by nesting further.

problems.title: Custom Collector — Running Statistics
problems.description: Implement a custom `Collector<Integer, ?, RunningStats>` that computes count, sum, min, max, and average in a single stream pass. `RunningStats` is a mutable accumulator. Use `Collector.of()`.
problems.hint1: RunningStats: fields count, sum, min, max. Supplier: `RunningStats::new`.
problems.hint2: Accumulator: `(stats, n) -> stats.accept(n)` — update all fields for each element.
problems.hint3: Combiner (for parallel): merge two RunningStats into one. Finisher: identity (RunningStats is the result).
problems.constraints: Non-empty list.
problems.editorial: Collector.of(supplier, accumulator, combiner, finisher, characteristics). IDENTITY_FINISH means the finisher is the identity function — the accumulator IS the result. The combiner merges two partial accumulators for parallel correctness. This avoids five separate terminal operations (five passes) by doing everything in one pass.

problems.title: Infinite Stream With limit()
problems.description: Use `Stream.iterate()` and `Stream.generate()` to create infinite streams. Generate: (1) Fibonacci numbers (first 10), (2) random integers between 1-100 (take first 5 that are > 50), (3) powers of 2 up to 1024.
problems.hint1: `Stream.iterate(new long[]{0,1}, f -> new long[]{f[1], f[0]+f[1]})` — state as array.
problems.hint3: `Stream.iterate(1, n -> n*2).takeWhile(n -> n <= 1024)` — Java 9+ takeWhile.
problems.constraints: Never collect an infinite stream without limit() or takeWhile().
problems.editorial: Stream.iterate(seed, fn) generates seed, fn(seed), fn(fn(seed))... infinitely. limit(n) caps it. Stream.generate(supplier) calls the supplier repeatedly. takeWhile(predicate) stops at the first false (Java 9+) — correct for monotonically changing sequences. Always pair infinite streams with a short-circuit: limit, takeWhile, findFirst, or anyMatch.

problems.title: Parallel Word Count
problems.description: Given a large list of sentences, count the total occurrences of each word across all sentences using a parallel stream pipeline. Return `Map<String, Long>`. Words should be lowercased and punctuation stripped. Compare sequential vs parallel performance.
problems.hint2: `Collectors.groupingByConcurrent` is thread-safe — required for parallel collect.
problems.hint3: Strip punctuation: `word.replaceAll("[^a-zA-Z]", "").toLowerCase()`. Filter blanks after stripping.
problems.constraints: Use groupingByConcurrent for parallel safety.
problems.editorial: groupingBy (non-concurrent) collects into a regular HashMap which is not thread-safe for parallel streams — use groupingByConcurrent which uses a ConcurrentHashMap. flatMap expands each sentence into individual words before the parallel collector processes them. The order of words in the map is non-deterministic but counts are correct.

problems.title: Stream Pipeline — SQL-Style JOIN
problems.description: Given `List<Order>(orderId, customerId, amount)` and `List<Customer>(customerId, name)`, simulate a SQL JOIN using streams: return `List<String>` of `'CustomerName: $amount'` for every order, sorted by amount descending.
problems.hint1: Build a `Map<Integer, String>` of customerId→name first (O(1) lookup).
problems.hint2: Stream orders, map each order to `customerMap.get(order.customerId) + ': $' + order.amount`.
problems.hint3: Sort by amount descending before collecting.
problems.constraints: No order references an unknown customer in this test. Handle with getOrDefault.
problems.editorial: The efficient stream join pattern: build a Map from the smaller collection first (O(n)), then stream the larger collection doing O(1) lookups. Never nested-loop join in streams — that would be O(n²). This is exactly how SQL hash joins work — build a hash table on the smaller side, probe it for each row of the larger side.

problems.title: Lazy Evaluation — peek() and Short-Circuit
problems.description: Demonstrate stream laziness: (1) use `peek()` to show which elements are actually processed when `findFirst()` is used. (2) Show that `limit()` stops iteration early. (3) Show that `count()` on a filtered stream does NOT short-circuit — it processes all elements.
problems.hint1: `peek(e -> System.out.println('processing: '+e))` logs elements that actually flow through.
problems.hint2: With findFirst(): only elements up to the first match are processed.
problems.hint3: count() after filter() processes every element — use `mapToLong(x->1L).sum()` — same result, same cost.
problems.constraints: Conceptual demonstration — output varies by peek() placement.
problems.editorial: peek() is a non-interfering, stateless intermediate op for debugging — it receives each element as it flows through. findFirst() is short-circuit: only elements 1, 2, 3 are tested for divisibility by 3; elements 4–10 are never touched. count() is NOT short-circuit — even though many filtered elements are discarded, the filter must test every element to know how many pass.

problems.title: Build a Query DSL With Method Chaining and Streams
problems.description: Implement a fluent `QueryBuilder<T>` that wraps a `List<T>` and provides chainable stream operations: `filter(Predicate)`, `map(Function)`, `sortBy(Comparator)`, `limit(int)`, `skip(int)`, `distinct()`. Execute with `toList()` or `count()`. Internally build and apply a stream pipeline.
problems.hint1: Store a `Stream<T>` field. Each method reassigns: `this.stream = stream.filter(pred); return this;`
problems.hint2: Return type of map() changes T: use a separate `QueryBuilder<R>` and wrap the mapped stream.
problems.hint3: toList() calls `stream.collect(Collectors.toList())`. count() calls `stream.count()`.
problems.constraints: A QueryBuilder can only be terminal-called once (streams are not reusable).
problems.editorial: Storing and reassigning the Stream<T> field is valid because stream operations return new stream objects. map() must return a new QueryBuilder<R> (different type parameter) rather than reassigning this. This pattern mirrors how Spring Data's QueryDSL Predicates, jOOQ's DSL, and even Java's own Stream API itself are implemented — a fluent builder that lazily accumulates operations and executes them at the terminal call.
